slides.title: Nature & Weather
bullets: Prepare to read, discuss and write
slides.narration: Welcome to Grade 4 English, Unit 2: Nature & Weather. Welcome to Unit 2! In this unit you will explore the natural world and the weather. You will learn to name places in nature — canyons, meadows and bays — and to talk about weather, from a foggy morning to a powerful hurricane. You will compare things (which storm is stronger?), describe facts about the world, and ask questions like a real scientist. Along the way you will read about weather, listen to a weather report, speak about the world around you, and write your own nature facts and poems. By the end, you will be ready to explain how weather works and how to stay safe — just like Noah and Sophia do in this unit's story. In this short lecture, preview the key ideas and prepare for the independent lesson.

slides.title: What you will achieve
bullets: Name and describe places in nature and different kinds of weather
Compare two things using comparative adjectives (deeper, stronger, foggier)
Say which is the greatest using superlative adjectives (the deepest, the most powerful)
slides.narration: By the end of this unit, you will be able to name and describe places in nature and different kinds of weather. you will also compare two things using comparative adjectives (deeper, stronger, foggier). you will also say which is the greatest using superlative adjectives (the deepest, the most powerful). Keep these goals in mind as you work.

slides.title: Language for precise thinking
slides.narration: Your vocabulary work begins with these words: breath, sweat, moisture, surface, fur, canyon. Listen to each pronunciation in the vocabulary lab, study its meaning in context, and use the word accurately in your own discussion and writing.

slides.title: Read critically
slides.narration: The reading sequence includes Weather Around the World and A Poem: “The Foggy Morning”. Read actively. Identify each text's central ideas, notice viewpoint and reliability, and support every inference with relevant evidence from the text.

slides.title: Comparative Adjectives
bullets: Comparative adjectives compare two things. They tell us how one thing is more or less than another — bigger, deeper, stronger. They help us describe our...
slides.narration: The first language focus is Comparative Adjectives. Comparative adjectives compare two things. They tell us how one thing is more or less than another — bigger, deeper, stronger. They help us describe our world clearly. For example: “A hurricane is stronger than a storm.” Notice the structure in the model examples, then apply it deliberately in connected sentences and paragraphs.

slides.title: Use English for a real purpose
bullets: Explain, support and improve your ideas
slides.narration: In speaking, you will complete Speaking 1: Describe Today's Weather. Organise your ideas, use evidence and respond clearly to questions. In writing, you will plan, draft, check and revise. Use the success criteria before submitting your work.

slides.title: You are ready to begin
slides.narration: You are ready to begin. Start with the vocabulary lab, continue through reading and comprehension, apply the grammar focus, and complete the speaking and writing practices. Use AI English for hints and feedback, and bring your questions to the live sessions.
